entry.title: Deploying a Serverless Function with OpenFaaS
entry.summary: Introduction Serverless computing allows developers to run code without managing the underlying infrastructure. Cloud providers handle provisioning, scaling, and maintenance, while developers focus on writing…
entry.content:
Introduction

Serverless computing allows developers to run code without managing the underlying infrastructure. Cloud providers handle provisioning, scaling, and maintenance, while developers focus on writing functions that execute in response to events. This post walks through deploying a simple serverless function using OpenFaaS, an open-source serverless platform that can run on top of Docker and Kubernetes.

Why OpenFaaS?

While managed serverless platforms like AWS Lambda and Azure Functions are widely used in production, OpenFaaS offers a provider-agnostic alternative that can run on any infrastructure where Docker or Kubernetes is available. This makes it a good option for learning how serverless works at a lower level, and for organisations that want to avoid vendor lock-in with their serverless workloads (OpenFaaS, 2024).

Setup

OpenFaaS was deployed locally using Docker Swarm. The faas-cli command-line tool was installed to manage functions.

 
bash
# Install faas-cli
curl -sL https://cli.openfaas.com | sudo sh

# Deploy OpenFaaS on Docker Swarm
docker swarm init
git clone https://github.com/openfaas/faas.git
cd faas
./deploy_stack.sh

# Log in to the OpenFaaS gateway
faas-cli login --password <password> --gateway http://127.0.0.1:8080

Creating the Function

I created a simple cloud cost estimator function that takes a number of virtual machines and returns an estimated monthly cost. This is a task I deal with regularly when scoping cloud solutions for clients.

bash
# Create a new function using the Python template
faas-cli new cost-estimator --lang python3

# This creates the following structure:
# cost-estimator/
#   handler.py
#   requirements.txt
# cost-estimator.yml

The function logic in handler.py:

import json

def handle(req):
    try:
        data = json.loads(req)
        vm_count = int(data.get("vm_count", 1))
        vm_size = data.get("vm_size", "standard")

        pricing = {
            "small": 25.00,
            "standard": 75.00,
            "large": 150.00
        }

        unit_cost = pricing.get(vm_size, 75.00)
        monthly_cost = vm_count * unit_cost

        return json.dumps({
            "vm_count": vm_count,
            "vm_size": vm_size,
            "unit_cost_usd": unit_cost,
            "estimated_monthly_cost_usd": monthly_cost
        })
    except Exception as e:
        return json.dumps({"error": str(e)})

Building and Deploying

 
bash
# Build the function container
faas-cli build -f cost-estimator.yml

# Deploy to OpenFaaS
faas-cli deploy -f cost-estimator.yml --gateway http://127.0.0.1:8080

Testing the Function

bash
# Invoke the function
echo '{"vm_count": 5, "vm_size": "standard"}' | faas-cli invoke cost-estimator

# Response:
# {"vm_count": 5, "vm_size": "standard", "unit_cost_usd": 75.0, "estimated_monthly_cost_usd": 375.0}

The function executed successfully, returning the cost estimate within milliseconds. OpenFaaS handled the container lifecycle automatically, spinning up the function on invocation and scaling it down when idle.

How Serverless Optimises Cloud Operations

Serverless architecture optimises cloud operations in several ways. First, it eliminates the need to provision and maintain servers. The platform handles scaling automatically based on demand, so resources are only consumed when the function is actually running. Second, the pay-per-invocation pricing model of managed serverless platforms means organisations only pay for the compute time they use, which can significantly reduce costs for workloads with variable traffic. Third, serverless functions are small and focused, making them easier to develop, test, and deploy independently compared to monolithic applications (Toosi et al., 2025).

The trade-off is that serverless introduces cold start latency when a function has not been invoked recently, and there are limits on execution duration and memory that may not suit all workloads. For long-running or compute-heavy tasks, traditional container-based deployments remain more appropriate.

References

OpenFaaS (2024) OpenFaaS documentation. Available at: https://docs.openfaas.com/

Toosi, A.N. et al. (2025) 'Serverless Computing for Next-Generation Application Development', Future Generation Computer Systems, 164, p. 107573.

entry.title: Migrating a MySQL Database to the Cloud Using Percona XtraBackup
entry.summary: Introduction Database migration is one of the most critical and risk-prone steps in any cloud migration project. This post walks through the process of migrating…
entry.content:
Introduction

Database migration is one of the most critical and risk-prone steps in any cloud migration project. This post walks through the process of migrating a MySQL database from a local machine to a cloud environment using Percona XtraBackup, an open-source backup tool designed for MySQL and MariaDB databases. The focus is on maintaining data consistency and minimising downtime during the migration.

Why Percona XtraBackup?

Percona XtraBackup performs hot backups of MySQL databases, meaning it can create a consistent backup without locking the database or interrupting read and write operations. This is important for production databases where downtime needs to be kept to a minimum. Unlike mysqldump, which creates a logical backup by exporting SQL statements, XtraBackup copies the actual data files at the physical level, making it faster for large databases (Percona, 2024).

Migration Steps

Step 1: Install Percona XtraBackup

Percona XtraBackup was installed on the source machine running MySQL 8.0 on Ubuntu:

sudo apt-get install percona-xtrabackup-80

Step 2: Create a Full Backup

A full backup of the local MySQL database was created:

xtrabackup --backup --target-dir=/data/backups/full \
  --user=root --password=<password>

This copies the InnoDB data files and transaction logs to the target directory without locking the database.

Step 3: Prepare the Backup

Before restoring, the backup needs to be prepared. This step applies any pending transactions captured in the log files to make the data files consistent:

xtrabackup --prepare --target-dir=/data/backups/full

Step 4: Transfer Backup to Cloud Instance

The prepared backup was transferred to the cloud-hosted MySQL instance using rsync over SSH:

rsync -avz /data/backups/full/ user@cloud-instance:/var/lib/mysql/

Step 5: Restore on the Cloud Instance

On the cloud instance, the MySQL data directory was replaced with the transferred backup files:

sudo systemctl stop mysql
sudo rsync -avz /data/backups/full/ /var/lib/mysql/
sudo chown -R mysql:mysql /var/lib/mysql
sudo systemctl start mysql

Step 6: Verify Data Consistency

After restoration, data consistency was verified by comparing row counts and checksums between the source and target databases:

# On source
mysql -e "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM app.orders;"

# On target
mysql -e "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM app.orders;"

Both returned matching results, confirming a successful migration.

Challenges Faced

Network transfer speed: For larger databases, transferring backup files over the network can be slow. Compressing the backup before transfer using gzip or pigz would reduce transfer time significantly.

Permission issues: After restoring the data directory on the cloud instance, MySQL failed to start because file ownership had changed during the transfer. This was resolved by resetting ownership to the mysql user.

Version compatibility: Percona XtraBackup must match the MySQL version on both source and target. A version mismatch between XtraBackup 8.0 and an older MySQL instance on the cloud caused an initial restore failure, which was resolved by upgrading the target instance.

References

Percona (2024) Percona XtraBackup documentation. Available at: https://docs.percona.com/percona-xtrabackup/

MySQL (2024) MySQL 8.0 Reference Manual. Available at: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.0/en/

entry.title: Designing a Disaster Recovery Plan Using Azure Site Recovery
entry.summary: 1. Introduction Disaster Recovery (DR) is about making sure that when something goes wrong, whether it is a hardware failure, a regional outage, or a…
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Disaster Recovery (DR) is about making sure that when something goes wrong, whether it is a hardware failure, a regional outage, or a ransomware attack, services can be restored quickly with minimal data loss. This post walks through the process of designing and implementing a DR plan for a cloud-hosted business application using Microsoft Azure Site Recovery (ASR).

While the module's focus has been on OpenStack-based environments, I chose to work with Azure for this exercise because it is the platform I use professionally to manage client infrastructure. The principles of DR planning, including defining recovery objectives, automating failover, and testing recovery procedures, apply regardless of the cloud platform (Carson and Rodrick, 2024).

2. Scenario

The scenario involves a small professional services firm running its core line-of-business application on Azure. The environment includes:

  • Two virtual machines (application server and database server) hosted in Azure UAE North
  • Azure SQL Database for structured data
  • Azure Blob Storage for document storage
  • Users accessing the application through a web frontend

The business requires the application to be recoverable within 1 hour (RTO) with no more than 15 minutes of data loss (RPO).

3. DR Strategy Design

Recovery Objectives

Objective Target
Recovery Time Objective (RTO) Less than 1 hour
Recovery Point Objective (RPO) Less than 15 minutes
DR Region Azure West Europe (secondary)

Components

Azure Site Recovery (ASR): Handles continuous replication of virtual machines from the primary region (UAE North) to the secondary region (West Europe). ASR replicates VM disks asynchronously, capturing changes every few minutes to meet the RPO target.

Azure SQL Geo-Replication: The Azure SQL Database is configured with active geo-replication to the secondary region. This provides an automatically updated readable secondary that can be promoted to primary during a failover.

Azure Blob Storage GRS: Blob storage is configured with Geo-Redundant Storage (GRS), which replicates data to the paired region. For faster failover, Read-Access GRS (RA-GRS) allows read access to the secondary copy before a full failover is triggered.

Azure Traffic Manager: A DNS-based traffic routing service configured with priority routing. Under normal conditions, all traffic goes to the primary region. If the health probe detects an outage, traffic is automatically redirected to the secondary region.

4. Implementation Process

Step 1: Enable ASR Replication

Azure Site Recovery was enabled in the Recovery Services vault. The two virtual machines were added to the replication policy with a replication frequency of 5 minutes. ASR automatically provisions placeholder resources (NICs, disks, availability sets) in the target region.

Step 2: Configure SQL Geo-Replication

Active geo-replication was configured on the Azure SQL Database through the Azure portal. The secondary database was created in West Europe with asynchronous replication. A failover group was set up to allow automatic failover if the primary becomes unreachable.

Step 3: Configure Storage Redundancy

The Blob Storage account was set to RA-GRS to ensure data is replicated to the paired region and remains readable during an outage.

Step 4: Set Up Traffic Manager

An Azure Traffic Manager profile was created with priority routing. The primary endpoint pointed to the UAE North frontend, and the secondary endpoint pointed to the West Europe resources. Health probes were configured to check application availability every 30 seconds.

Step 5: Create a Recovery Plan

A recovery plan was built in ASR that defines the order of operations during failover. The database server starts first, followed by the application server, ensuring dependencies are respected. Custom scripts were added to update connection strings and verify service health after failover.

5. Testing the DR Plan

A test failover was executed using ASR's built-in test failover feature. This creates the recovered VMs in an isolated virtual network in the secondary region without affecting production. The test confirmed:

  • Both VMs started successfully in the correct order
  • The application connected to the geo-replicated SQL database
  • Users could access the application through the Traffic Manager endpoint
  • Recovery completed in approximately 40 minutes, within the 1-hour RTO target

6. Reflection

What worked well

  • ASR's continuous replication and automated failover made the process straightforward compared to manual backup and restore approaches
  • The recovery plan feature ensured that VMs started in the right dependency order
  • Test failover allowed validation without impacting production

Challenges

  • Initial setup required careful configuration of networking in the secondary region to match the primary
  • SQL geo-replication added latency to write operations, which needed to be communicated to the application team
  • Cost of maintaining replicated resources in a secondary region is an ongoing expense that needs to be justified against the business impact of downtime

Comparison with open-source approaches

Tools like Duplicity and Bacula, which my peers explored using OpenStack, offer cost-effective DR for environments where managed services are not available. However, they require more manual effort during recovery and lack the automated failover capabilities that ASR provides. The trade-off is between cost and recovery speed, and the right choice depends on the organisation's budget and RTO requirements.

7. Conclusion

This exercise demonstrated that a well-designed DR plan using Azure Site Recovery can meet tight recovery objectives with relatively low operational effort. The key takeaway is that DR planning is not just about having backups. It requires automated replication, defined recovery procedures, and regular testing to be effective (Carson and Rodrick, 2024).

References

Carson, R. and Rodrick, M. (2024) Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery. Birmingham: Packt Publishing.

Microsoft (2024) Azure Site Recovery overview. Available at: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/site-recovery/site-recovery-overview

Microsoft (2024) Active geo-replication for Azure SQL Database. Available at: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-sql/database/active-geo-replication-overview

entry.title: Security Audit of an OpenStack Cloud Application Using OpenVAS
entry.summary: 1. Introduction This post presents the findings from a mock security audit of a cloud application deployed on OpenStack. The audit uses OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability…
entry.content:
1. Introduction

This post presents the findings from a mock security audit of a cloud application deployed on OpenStack. The audit uses OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment Scanner) to identify vulnerabilities across the cloud infrastructure. Each finding is mapped to relevant ISO/IEC 27001 controls, and mitigation strategies are recommended.

OpenStack provides a flexible open-source platform for private and hybrid cloud deployments, but this flexibility also means that security responsibility falls heavily on the operator. Regular vulnerability assessments help ensure that misconfigurations and known weaknesses are identified before they can be exploited (OpenStack Foundation, 2023).

2. Audit Scope and Methodology

Target systems: OpenStack controller node, two compute nodes hosting a multi-tier web application, and associated networking and storage services.

Scanning approach: OpenVAS was configured to run both authenticated and unauthenticated scans across the internal management network and the tenant application network. Authenticated scans used SSH credentials on the compute nodes to assess package-level vulnerabilities. Scan results were classified using CVSS v3 severity scores and mapped to ISO/IEC 27001:2022 controls.

3. Vulnerabilities Identified

3.1 Unrestricted API Access to Keystone Endpoints

Severity: High

The Keystone identity service API was accessible from the tenant network without IP restrictions. OpenVAS identified the admin API endpoint responding to unauthenticated requests from any network segment.

Risk: An attacker with access to the tenant network could attempt token-based attacks or enumerate user accounts through the exposed admin API.

Mitigation: Restrict Keystone admin API access to the management network only using Neutron security groups and firewall rules. Separate the admin and public API endpoints onto different network interfaces.

ISO/IEC 27001 Control: A.8.20 (Network security)

3.2 Missing Disk Encryption on Compute Nodes

Severity: High

OpenVAS flagged that the block storage volumes attached to compute instances were not using encryption. Data written to Cinder volumes was stored in plaintext on the underlying storage backend.

Risk: If physical storage media is compromised or decommissioned without proper wiping, sensitive application data could be recovered.

Mitigation: Enable Cinder volume encryption using LUKS with keys managed through Barbican (OpenStack Key Manager). Apply encryption as the default for all new volumes through Cinder volume type policies.

ISO/IEC 27001 Control: A.8.24 (Use of cryptography)

3.3 Stale User Accounts in Keystone

Severity: Medium

The authenticated scan identified several Keystone user accounts that had not been used in over 90 days but retained active project roles, including two accounts with admin-level privileges.

Risk: Dormant accounts with elevated privileges are a common target for credential-based attacks. If compromised, they provide an attacker with legitimate access that is difficult to detect.

Mitigation: Implement an account lifecycle policy that disables accounts after 60 days of inactivity. Conduct quarterly access reviews to remove unnecessary role assignments. Where possible, integrate Keystone with a centralised identity provider to manage account provisioning and deprovisioning.

ISO/IEC 27001 Control: A.5.18 (Access rights)

3.4 Outdated OpenStack Services

Severity: Medium

Several OpenStack services on the controller node were running versions two releases behind the current stable branch. OpenVAS matched these against known CVEs including vulnerabilities in the Nova API and Neutron L3 agent.

Risk: Known vulnerabilities in outdated services can be exploited using publicly available tools and scripts.

Mitigation: Establish a patch management schedule aligned with OpenStack release cycles. Test updates in a staging environment before applying to production. Subscribe to the OpenStack Security Advisories (OSSA) mailing list for early notification of critical patches.

ISO/IEC 27001 Control: A.8.8 (Management of technical vulnerabilities)

3.5 No Centralised Log Aggregation

Severity: Medium

OpenStack services were writing logs locally on each node. There was no centralised log collection or correlation, and no alerting for security-relevant events such as repeated authentication failures or security group modifications.

Risk: Without centralised logging, detecting and investigating security incidents becomes slow and unreliable. Attackers can also tamper with local log files to cover their tracks.

Mitigation: Deploy a centralised logging solution such as ELK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) or Graylog. Forward all OpenStack service logs and system audit logs to the central store. Configure alert rules for high-priority events including failed Keystone authentications and changes to security group rules.

ISO/IEC 27001 Control: A.8.15 (Logging)

4. Summary of Findings

# Finding Severity ISO 27001 Control
1 Unrestricted Keystone API access High A.8.20
2 Missing disk encryption High A.8.24
3 Stale user accounts Medium A.5.18
4 Outdated OpenStack services Medium A.8.8
5 No centralised logging Medium A.8.15

5. Compliance Recommendations

To improve alignment with ISO/IEC 27001:

  1. Establish a formal vulnerability management process with scheduled OpenVAS scans.
  2. Implement encryption for data at rest across all storage services.
  3. Enforce account lifecycle management with periodic access reviews.
  4. Maintain a patching schedule tied to OpenStack security advisories.
  5. Deploy centralised logging with automated alerting for security events.

6. Conclusion

This audit identified five vulnerabilities ranging from high to medium severity. The findings highlight common security gaps in OpenStack deployments, particularly around API exposure, encryption, identity management, patching, and logging. Addressing these issues through the recommended mitigations would significantly strengthen the security posture and move the environment closer to compliance with ISO/IEC 27001.

References

Greenbone Networks (2024) OpenVAS: Open Vulnerability Assessment Scanner. Available at: https://www.openvas.org/

ISO (2022) ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Information security management systems. Available at: https://www.iso.org/standard/27001

OpenStack Foundation (2023) OpenStack Security Guide. Available at: https://docs.openstack.org/security-guide/

entry.title: Setting Up a Local Kubernetes Cluster with Minikube
entry.summary: Introduction As someone who works with Azure Container Apps daily, I have been using managed container orchestration without needing to think much about the underlying…
entry.content:
Introduction

As someone who works with Azure Container Apps daily, I have been using managed container orchestration without needing to think much about the underlying Kubernetes layer. This activity gave me the opportunity to set up a local Kubernetes cluster using Minikube and get hands-on with the core components that platforms like Azure abstract away.

What is Minikube?

Minikube is a lightweight tool that runs a single-node Kubernetes cluster on your local machine. It is designed for learning and development purposes, allowing you to experiment with Kubernetes without needing a cloud subscription or a multi-node setup. It supports multiple container runtimes including Docker and containerd, and runs inside a virtual machine or container on your workstation.

Setting Up the Cluster

I set this up on my Dell XPS 17 running Windows with Docker Desktop already installed. The steps were straightforward.

First, I installed Minikube using the Windows installer:

# Download and install Minikube
winget install Kubernetes.minikube

Then I started the cluster using Docker as the driver:

# Start Minikube with Docker driver
minikube start --driver=docker

# Verify the cluster is running
minikube status

# Check the node is ready
kubectl get nodes

The output confirmed a single node running with the roles of control-plane and master. From here I deployed a simple Nginx web server to test the cluster:

# Create a deployment
kubectl create deployment nginx-test --image=nginx

# Expose it as a service
kubectl expose deployment nginx-test --type=NodePort --port=80

# Get the URL to access it
minikube service nginx-test --url

This gave me a local URL where I could access the Nginx welcome page in my browser, confirming the full pipeline was working: cluster running, pod scheduled, service exposed.

I also explored the Minikube dashboard, which provides a web-based UI to view deployments, pods, services, and logs:

minikube dashboard

Setting up Minikube helped me understand what is happening beneath the managed services I use in production. When I deploy containers to Azure Container Apps, Azure handles the Kubernetes orchestration behind the scenes. Working with Minikube directly exposed the building blocks: nodes, pods, deployments, services, and the kubectl command-line interface that ties them together.

The key concepts I took away from this exercise are worth noting. Pods are the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, wrapping one or more containers. Deployments manage the desired state of pods, handling scaling and updates automatically. Services provide stable networking endpoints so that other applications or users can reach the running pods. These are the same primitives that power production Kubernetes clusters at scale, just running locally on a single node.

One thing that stood out was how quickly Minikube lets you iterate. I could create a deployment, break it on purpose, watch Kubernetes restart the failed pod, and see self-healing in action. This is something I rely on in production with Azure but had never seen at the Kubernetes level directly.

From an MSP perspective, understanding these fundamentals matters. Not all clients use managed services. Some run their own Kubernetes clusters on-premises or in hybrid setups, and being able to speak to the core concepts makes a difference when designing solutions or troubleshooting issues.

Conclusion

Minikube is a practical tool for learning Kubernetes without the cost or complexity of a cloud-hosted cluster. This exercise bridged the gap between the managed container services I use professionally and the orchestration layer that powers them. Going forward, I plan to use Minikube as a local testing environment before pushing container configurations to Azure.

References

Kubernetes (2024) Minikube: Local Kubernetes. Available at: https://minikube.sigs.k8s.io/docs/

Kubernetes (2024) kubectl Cheat Sheet. Available at: https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/cheatsheet/

Nigel, P. and Pushkar, J. (2017) The Kubernetes Book. Leanpub.

entry.title: Automating Cloud Deployment with Bash: A Real-World Azure CLI Script
entry.summary: Introduction This post presents a Bash script I wrote and use in production to automate the build and deployment of a multi-container application on Microsoft…
entry.content:
Introduction

This post presents a Bash script I wrote and use in production to automate the build and deployment of a multi-container application on Microsoft Azure. The script provisions cloud resources using Azure CLI commands and is part of the deployment workflow for an MSP platform that runs on Azure Container Apps with images stored in Azure Container Registry (ACR).

The full script is included below, followed by an explanation of how it works and why automation through scripting is important for cloud operations.

The Script

 
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Fast local deploy to Azure using ACR Tasks — no local Docker needed.
# Source context is uploaded to ACR and built in the cloud.
#
# Usage:
#   ./deploy-local.sh           — auto-detect changes and deploy affected containers
#   ./deploy-local.sh --admin   — force admin only
#   ./deploy-local.sh --portal  — force portal only
#   ./deploy-local.sh --agent   — force agent only
#   ./deploy-local.sh --all     — force everything
#   ./deploy-local.sh --full    — force full rebuild including Go
#   ./deploy-local.sh --dry-run — show what would deploy without doing it

set -euo pipefail

# Config
ACR_NAME="acrnshield2"
ACR="${ACR_NAME}.azurecr.io"
IMAGE="${ACR}/mspcc"
RESOURCE_GROUP="rg-nshield-prod"
ADMIN_APP="ca-nshield-prod"
PORTAL_APP="ca-nshield-portal"
AGENT_APP="ca-nshield-agent"
SHA_FILE=".last-deployed-sha"

# Parse flags
MODE="auto"
DRY_RUN=false
FORCE_FULL=false

for arg in "$@"; do
  case $arg in
    --admin)   MODE="admin" ;;
    --portal)  MODE="portal" ;;
    --agent)   MODE="agent" ;;
    --all)     MODE="all" ;;
    --full)    FORCE_FULL=true; MODE="all" ;;
    --dry-run) DRY_RUN=true ;;
  esac
done

# Change detection using git diff
detect_changes() {
  local last_sha=""
  if [ -f "$SHA_FILE" ]; then
    last_sha=$(cat "$SHA_FILE")
  fi

  if [ -z "$last_sha" ]; then
    echo "==> No previous deploy SHA found"
    CHANGED_BACKEND=true
    CHANGED_FRONTEND=true
    CHANGED_MIGRATIONS=true
    return
  fi

  echo "==> Detecting changes since last deploy (${last_sha:0:7}..HEAD)"
  local files
  files=$(git diff --name-only "$last_sha" HEAD 2>/dev/null || true)
  local uncommitted
  uncommitted=$(git diff --name-only HEAD 2>/dev/null || true)
  local staged
  staged=$(git diff --name-only --cached 2>/dev/null || true)
  files=$(printf '%s\n%s\n%s' "$files" "$uncommitted" "$staged" | sort -u | grep -v '^$' || true)

  while IFS= read -r file; do
    case "$file" in
      src/db/migrations/*|src/db/seeds/*)  CHANGED_MIGRATIONS=true; CHANGED_BACKEND=true ;;
      src/*)                                CHANGED_BACKEND=true ;;
      apps/web/*)                           CHANGED_FRONTEND=true ;;
      agents/rmm/*)                         CHANGED_AGENT=true ;;
      package.json|package-lock.json)       CHANGED_DEPS=true ;;
      Dockerfile)                           CHANGED_DOCKER=true ;;
      *)                                    CHANGED_BACKEND=true ;;
    esac
  done <<< "$files"
}

# Build in ACR and deploy to Azure Container Apps
TAG="local-$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)-$(date +%s)"

az acr build \
  --registry "${ACR_NAME}" \
  --image "mspcc:${TAG}" \
  --image "mspcc:latest" \
  .

# Deploy to each container app as needed
az containerapp update \
  --name "${ADMIN_APP}" \
  --resource-group "${RESOURCE_GROUP}" \
  --image "${IMAGE}:${TAG}"

git rev-parse HEAD > "$SHA_FILE"
echo "Done. Image: ${IMAGE}:${TAG}"

Note: The script above is a simplified version. The full production script includes additional logic for selective deployment, agent builds, and dry-run mode.

How the Script Works

The script automates the full build and deployment cycle for a cloud application running on Azure Container Apps. It starts by checking which parts of the codebase have changed since the last deployment. It does this by comparing the current Git HEAD against a stored SHA from the previous deploy. Based on the files that changed, it maps those changes to the correct containers. For example, changes to backend source files trigger an admin container rebuild, while frontend changes trigger both admin and portal container updates.

Once the targets are identified, the script builds a Docker image using Azure Container Registry (ACR) Tasks. This means the build happens in the cloud rather than on the local machine, which saves time and avoids the need for Docker to be installed locally. The built image is tagged with the Git commit hash and a timestamp for traceability.

After the build completes, the script uses Azure CLI to update the relevant Azure Container Apps with the new image. It handles three separate container apps: admin, portal, and agent. Each one is only updated if its corresponding source files changed, which avoids unnecessary deployments.

Why Scripting Matters for Cloud Operations

Automation through scripting is essential for modern cloud operations for several reasons.

First, it removes the risk of human error. Manual deployments require running multiple commands in the right order, which is easy to get wrong, especially under pressure. A script runs the same steps every time, exactly as defined.

Second, scripting saves time. What would take 15 to 20 minutes of manual work is reduced to a single command that completes in a few minutes. This is especially important when deploying frequently during development cycles.

Third, scripts provide a record of what happened. By storing the deployed SHA and tagging images with commit hashes, the script creates a clear audit trail. If something goes wrong, it is straightforward to identify which code was deployed and roll back.

Fourth, selective deployment based on change detection reduces cloud costs. Instead of rebuilding and redeploying everything on each change, the script only targets the containers that are actually affected. This reduces build time, network usage, and container restart overhead.

Finally, scripting supports collaboration. The deployment process is defined in code, stored in version control, and can be reviewed by the team. This is far more reliable than relying on documentation or tribal knowledge about how to deploy.

In summary, this script demonstrates how Bash and Azure CLI can be combined to automate cloud resource provisioning and deployment in a way that is repeatable, efficient, and traceable.

References

Microsoft (2024) Azure CLI documentation. Available at: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/

Microsoft (2024) Azure Container Apps overview. Available at: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-apps/overview

Microsoft (2024) Azure Container Registry Tasks. Available at: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/container-registry-tasks-overview

entry.title: Terraform - Superior Cloud Design Tool
entry.summary: Terraform is an open-source Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool developed by HashiCorp that allows teams to define, provision, and manage cloud infrastructure using declarative configuration…
entry.content:
Terraform is an open-source Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool developed by HashiCorp that allows teams to define, provision, and manage cloud infrastructure using declarative configuration files. In my view, Terraform is the superior cloud design tool because of its multi-cloud support, state management, and ability to create repeatable infrastructure across different environments.

The first reason Terraform stands out is its cloud-agnostic nature. Unlike AWS CloudFormation, which only works within the AWS ecosystem, Terraform supports AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and many other providers through a single configuration language called HCL (Jayaram et al., 2024). This is especially important for organisations that work across multiple cloud platforms or want to avoid being locked into a single vendor. In an MSP context where different clients may use different providers, this flexibility is essential.

Second, Terraform uses a declarative approach where users define what the infrastructure should look like rather than writing step-by-step instructions to build it. Terraform then calculates the changes needed to reach the desired state. This reduces the chance of human error and makes infrastructure easier to understand and review (Hafner, 2025). The state file that Terraform maintains acts as a single source of truth, tracking what has been deployed and detecting any drift from the expected configuration.

Third, Terraform integrates well with CI/CD pipelines and version control systems like Git. This means infrastructure changes can be reviewed, tested, and approved in the same way as application code. Feitosa et al. (2024) found that developers working with Terraform actively consider cost implications in their configurations, showing that IaC is not just about automation but also about making informed decisions during deployment.

In conclusion, Terraform's multi-cloud support, declarative model, and strong state management make it a practical and reliable choice for managing modern cloud infrastructure at scale.

References

Feitosa, D., Penca, M., Berardi, M., Boza, R.D. and Andrikopoulos, V. (2024) 'Mining for cost awareness in the infrastructure as code artifacts of cloud-based applications: An exploratory study', Journal of Systems and Software, 215, 112112. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jss.2024.112112

Hafner, R. (2025) Terraform in Depth: Infrastructure As Code with Terraform and OpenTofu. 1st edn. New York: Manning Publications.

Jayaram, V., Sankiti, S.R., Krishnappa, M.S., Veerapaneni, P.K. and Carimireddy, P.K. (2024) 'Accelerated Cloud Infrastructure Development Using Terraform', Journal of Emerging Technologies and Innovative Research, 11(9), pp. f382.

entry.title: Navigating the Cloud Service Model Landscape
entry.summary: As organisations move away from on-premises IT, it is important to understand cloud service models. Choosing between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS is not just a…
entry.content:
As organisations move away from on-premises IT, it is important to understand cloud service models. Choosing between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS is not just a technical decision. It is a strategic one. This choice affects how much control an organisation has, who is responsible for security, and how quickly services can be delivered. The main difference between these models is the shared responsibility model. It explains which security tasks are handled by the cloud provider and which remain the responsibility of the customer.

1. Core Differences Between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS

Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)

IaaS provides basic computing resources such as virtual machines, storage, and networking. The cloud provider secures the physical data centres and the underlying infrastructure, while the customer builds and manages everything above this layer. This includes the operating system, middleware, applications, and data. IaaS offers the highest level of control and flexibility, but it also comes with the greatest operational effort and security responsibility for the customer

Examples include Amazon Web Services EC2, Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines, and Google Cloud Compute Engine (AWS, 2023; Microsoft, 2024).

Platform as a Service (PaaS)

PaaS removes the need to manage servers and operating systems. It provides a managed environment where applications can be built and deployed quickly. The customer focuses on application code and data, while the cloud provider manages the platform, runtime, and operating system, including patching and maintenance. This model improves developer productivity and reduces setup time. Security responsibilities are shared, with the customer responsible for securing the application and its data.

Common examples include Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, and Heroku (Google, 2024; AWS, 2023).

Software as a Service (SaaS)

SaaS delivers ready to use applications over the internet that users access through a web browser or client application. The cloud provider manages the entire technology stack, including infrastructure, platforms, and application updates. The customer focuses on managing users, access controls, and how data is used within the service. SaaS provides the fastest time to value and requires the least operational effort. Customisation is limited, but reliability and scalability are typically high.

Examples include Microsoft 365, Salesforce, and Slack (Microsoft, 2024).

2. Organisational Impact and Strategic Choice

Each service model shifts responsibility and risk.

Factor

IaaS

PaaS

SaaS

Control

Highest level of control

Limited to application

Minimal control

Cost

Lower capital cost, higher skills cost

Balanced cost model

Predictable subscription cost

Speed

Slowest to deploy

Faster development

Immediate availability

Security

Customer secures OS and above

Shared at application level

Vendor secures most layers

In IaaS, security misconfigurations are a common risk.

In PaaS, insecure code becomes the primary threat.

In SaaS, identity and access management are the main concerns (NIST, 2023)

3. Why Organisations Choose Different Models

Organisations choose PaaS when software development is central to their business because it enables faster application delivery without the overhead of managing infrastructure. SaaS is commonly used for standard business functions such as email, HR, and collaboration tools, as it removes routine operational work and reduces maintenance effort. IaaS remains important for legacy systems and regulated workloads, where greater control is required to meet specific compliance, security, or architectural needs.

References

Amazon Web Services (2023) Shared Responsibility Model. Available at: https://aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model

Google Cloud (2024) PaaS overview and responsibilities. Available at: https://cloud.google.com/learn/paas

Microsoft (2024) Microsoft cloud shared responsibility model. Available at: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/shared-responsibility

NIST (2023) SP 800-210: Cloud Computing Security Considerations. National Institute of Standards and Technology. Available at: https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-210/final

entry.title: AWS vs Google Cloud - Comparison and Contrast
entry.summary: Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud Platform (GCP) are two leading public cloud providers. Both offer computing, storage, networking, and managed services. Both support scaling and…
entry.content:
Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud Platform (GCP) are two leading public cloud providers. Both offer computing, storage, networking, and managed services. Both support scaling and on-demand access to resources (Borra, 2024). 

AWS is known for its broad service catalogue and flexibility. It has many products for virtual machines, databases, analytics, and application services. This means users can select from a range of architectural design options. AWS supports complex enterprise use cases well because it has more regions and service maturity (Borra, 2024). 

GCP offers a robust platform for data analytics and cloud native development. It integrates well with Kubernetes and container workloads. Google’s services like BigQuery and Kubernetes Engine are easy to use and scale, making GCP suitable for modern applications and data-heavy workloads (Borra, 2024). 

Pricing models differ between them. AWS uses pay-as-you-go billing with many discount plans such as Reserved Instances and Savings Plans (CloudZero, 2025). GCP uses automatic sustained use and committed use discounts that can make costs more predictable for long-running workloads (CloudZero, 2025). 

In summary, AWS offers greater flexibility, a wide range of services, and robust enterprise support. GCP provides simpler pricing and strengthens analytics, machine learning, and container-based use cases. The choice depends on technical needs, budget, and expertise.

References:

Borra, P., 2024. Comparison and Analysis of Leading Cloud Service Providers (AWS, Azure and GCP), International Journal of Advanced Research in Engineering and Technology, 15(3), pp.266-278. Available at https://iaeme.com/MasterAdmin/Journal_uploads/IJARET/VOLUME_15_ISSUE_3/IJARET_15_03_023.pdf

CloudZero, 2025. AWS Vs. GCP: Which Platform Offers Better Pricing?.Available at: https://cast.ai/blog/cloud-pricing-comparison/

entry.title: DR Solutions Design and Review
entry.summary: Based on Kumar (2024) and Corbari et al. (2024) According to Kumar (2024), vendor lock-in manifests primarily through Technical and Organizational obstacles. Mitigation Strategies: To…
entry.content:
Based on Kumar (2024) and Corbari et al. (2024)

1. What are some of the main vendor lock-in issues the authors identify? How would you mitigate them?

According to Kumar (2024), vendor lock-in manifests primarily through Technical and Organizational obstacles.

  • Technical Issues: The use of proprietary APIs and non-standard data formats creates high switching costs. Once an organization integrates deeply with a specific cloud provider’s ecosystem (e.g., using AWS Lambda or proprietary databases), migrating to a different DR provider becomes technically prohibitive due to compatibility issues.
  • Organizational/Legal Issues: Restrictive contracts and the lack of interoperability standards further bind organizations to a single vendor.

Mitigation Strategies: To mitigate these risks, I would recommend a Multi-Cloud Strategy combined with Containerization (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes). By abstracting the application layer from the underlying infrastructure, organizations can move workloads between providers with minimal friction. Additionally, enforcing the use of Open Standards and avoiding proprietary PaaS (Platform as a Service) features where possible ensures that the DR solution remains portable.

2. What are some security concerns with the modern cloud? How can these be mitigated?

A major security concern in modern cloud environments is the loss of visibility and control over the underlying infrastructure. However, a more subtle but critical concern identified by Corbari et al. (2024) is the complexity of dependencies. In complex cloud environments, it is difficult to identify exactly which assets are critical to a specific business function. If a DR plan fails to account for a hidden dependency (e.g., an external authentication service), the recovery will fail.

Mitigation Strategies:

  • Mission Thread Analysis (MTA): I would apply the framework proposed by Corbari et al. (2024) to map the "Mission Relevant Cyber Terrain." This process involves tracing a specific operational thread (e.g., "Process Customer Payment") end-to-end to identify every critical node and link.
  • Shared Responsibility Awareness: Organizations must clearly define where the vendor's security responsibility ends and theirs begins, particularly regarding data encryption and access control.

References

  • Corbari, G.I., Khatod, N., Popiak, J.F. and Sinclair, P. (2024) ‘Mission Thread Analysis: Establishing a Common Framework’, The Cyber Defense Review, 9(1), pp. 37–54.
  • Kumar, A. (2024) Cloud Vendor Lock-In: Identify, Strategies and Mitigate. Seminar Paper, Julius-Maximilians-Universität Würzburg.
